title: AI Dev 26 x SF | Andi Partovi: Why Every Agent Needs a Simulation Sandbox

So you can see the impact of these agents much higher than anything like a co-pilot. But also you can imagine there's a lot of risk associated with that. So what we helped do and u what I want to do in this talk is talk about how you can make your agents more robust to regulations to internal policies um and how you can make sure that the agent is good it's working before going to production and making mistakes there. Um, I'm sure there's no hallucination, there's no big lawsuit coming. Uh the agents are usually typically fine. Uh no no curveball from customers, no databases completely being deleted. Um and and the sad part is actually a lot of these teams probably tested the agent beforehand as well. So it's not like they just shipped it. But there are challenges with evaluating and testing agents with the methods that we are all used to. uh methods that we used either as software engineers in the past or methods that we used as an machine learning expert in the past. You know, golden data sets, assertions, unit tests, uh evaluation data sets. Um agents are very different and the biggest things would be a couple of items I'll talk about now. One is that they are nondeterministic. Uh you can say the same thing, get different answers every time. So I'll put forward that any testing system that you use for AI agents need to do this at scale and at repetition because you see something once that does not guarantee that that would happen in production. You want to see that in scale you want to see out of the many times that this input is received this is the type of output we're going to get. The other aspect of it is that tests need to be interactive. uh we're going to go away from the world of golden data sets when we had inputs outputs associated with it. Uh to give you an example, we work with a customer. They're building what we call a sourcing agent. They're a supply chain uh chain customer and the sourcing agent goes out to the internet. Gets uh quotes about when when is this piece available, how many of them you have. It even negotiates over emails. It's great. But you can see that this is a very interactive test. If you want to test it out, you need a system that can receive emails, can send emails back. You need databases on the other side to answer. It's no longer about a static data set, input output pairs. Um, and and all of that is part of the agentic or multi- aent system. and labels are dynamic. If this is the case, this is true. This is right. Um, agents are dynamic. Uh, as we said, they're nondeterministic. They're interactive, which means that beforehand, you would not know what's the correct answer looks like. Um, if if my agent is supposed to move money from account A to account B, I can make an input request of move money and then make an assertion that if my account A is reduced by this amount or account B is increased by that amount, that's a correct action. Now, let's say the agent gets the request and tries to do the transaction, but then it's try to authenticate me. You know, if an agent is playing chess, they always know what the opponent is doing. But an AI agent on the other hand, it's in a what we call a POM DP or partially observable MDP where the actions of the environment is not fully known. You don't know the intentions of the users. You don't know what's the state of the systems that the agent is touching. Um so the way these type of agents interact with their environment is through an action and reward system. So in the sourcing agent example I mentioned earlier, all the vendors that the agent is interacting with are actors. Each of them have their own agenda. Each of them have their own inventory of items, pricing, negotiation tactics. Each of them had their own ideas of when their items would be available in inventory. Um how much they're going to share information with the agent, all of those things. Um and then the other thing is test scenarios. So the test scenarios are dictating how the simulation would run under different situations because the whole idea here is to create as many failure modes as you can to see the problems before going to production.
